Provisioning for Wilderness Marches

A practical guide for quartermasters, scouts, and prudent captains.

The inexperienced commander imagines that steel and courage are the measure of a campaign. Veterans know better. A company marches no farther than its waterskins, and no faster than the slowest mule under hard load. The wise planner therefore reckons provisions before banners, weather before war songs, and coin before glory. If this seems dull to the boastful, let the boastful learn hunger by the third day of dry road and take the lesson in silence.

Terrain governs appetite and thirst in unequal ways. A level road across temperate grassland permits disciplined pacing and regular halts, while broken upland demands labor from both leg and lung. Marsh travel compounds effort through wet footing, detours, and spoiled stores. Desert, most infamous of all, extracts water with pitiless arithmetic. It is not enough to carry the same sack of meal from one region to another and hope the gods provide.

Scribes often ask whether ration standards should account for rank. In this referee's judgment, no advantage of birth exempts one from heat, wind, and distance. Officers may dine better in camp, but on the march each mouth consumes and each skin empties. Distinctions in supply belong to special burdens only: armor weight, medical satchels, drafting tools, and other practical exceptions that can be named, counted, and defended in ledger.

Table 7: Weekly Provisions by Terrain (Per Adventurer)

Terrain Water (gal/day) Rations (lb/day) Weekly Cost (gp)
Plains, Temperate 1.2 1.6 3.4
Forest, Mixed 1.5 1.7 3.9
Hills and Broken Paths 1.7 1.8 4.3
Mountain Ascent 2.1 2.0 5.1
Swamp or Fen 1.9 1.9 4.8
Desert, Dry Wind 3.0 2.1 6.7

Use the table as a baseline, then increase totals for extraordinary burdens: forced march, sustained shield use, heavy mining, prolonged cold, or repeated climbing under armor. Keep one reserve day in compact form whenever pack space allows. Salted strips and hard biscuit travel well, and though they weary the tongue, they spare the company from desperate arithmetic at dusk.

Do not trust stream water by appearance alone. Clear flow can conceal rot from upstream camps, and still pools in hot weather betray the careless more often than the wicked. Boiling is slow, but slower still is burying two scouts before noon. A prudent trainmaster assigns one kettle to purification and one to common stew, and marks both by habit so fools do not reverse them.

Table 8: March Rate by Load Class

Load Class Typical Carried Weight (lb) Daily March Distance (miles) Fatigue Penalty
Light 0-25 18 None
Standard 26-45 15 -1 after 8 hrs
Heavy 46-65 12 -2 after 6 hrs
Overburdened 66+ 8 -4 after 4 hrs

Lastly, remember that provisioning is not merely subtraction from sacks and casks. It is also the craft of preserving morale through predictability. Regular issue at regular halts steadies both temper and pace. Men forgive hard roads sooner than they forgive uncertain supper. A captain who masters this plain truth may find his company willing on the eighth day where another's mutters on the fourth.

Account also for loss by weather and mishap. A torn grain sack, a flooded ford, or one startled mule can erase a careful estimate in an afternoon. For this reason, quartermasters of reputation divide critical stores among several packs instead of trusting all to one beast or cart. This rule seems cautious until the day it becomes the difference between inconvenience and disaster.

Where local markets are uncertain, carry trade goods suited to rough exchange: needles, lamp wick, common salt, and small iron fittings. Villages that cannot spare coin may still bargain in cheese, dried fruit, or grain meal. Such exchanges should be recorded at once, with witness if available, for memory grows generous after supper and strict at audit.

In winter travel, fuel may outrank flour for short intervals, especially in wind-scoured country where exposure drains strength before hunger does. A sensible leader sets watch rotations that protect both fire and sleepers, then adjusts morning departure by actual readiness rather than pride. Better one honest delay than three leagues of stumbling march that consume twice the ration and yield half the distance.

Chapter Nine: Resupply Along the Route

No sensible plan survives contact with a fortnight of mud, missing waypoints, and pack animals that decide to be philosophical. Resupply is therefore not a luxury reserved for long campaigns; it is the ordinary arithmetic of any march that lasts beyond a week. The quartermaster who leaves base camp with every provision for the entire journey invites the kind of ruin that makes for cautionary ballads rather than monuments.

Resupply nodes fall into three rough categories: friendly garrisons, settled civilian markets, and field caches established before the march begins. Each carries different assumptions about reliability, price, and speed of execution. A garrison may provide on written authority where a market demands coin, and a buried cache makes no demands at all except that someone remembered to dig it in the right clearing three weeks earlier.

When scheduling resupply, calculate backward from the node, not forward from departure. Determine the latest acceptable day of arrival, subtract travel time plus one full day of margin, and post the requirement notice on that date without fail. Quartermasters who calculate forward tend to arrive at the node one day early with optimism and two days late with excuses. Backward planning converts hope into obligation and obligation into habits of mind that survive the first unexpected river ford.

Civilian markets deserve particular caution in border regions. Prices fluctuate with rumor as readily as with scarcity, and a column of armed men purchases at rates that reflect the seller's assessment of their mood. Send a small party in plain traveling clothes a half-day ahead if circumstances allow. They will return with accurate prices, accurate stock information, and occasionally an invitation to dine that the main column would never receive. This is not deception; it is tact, which admirable commanders practice without embarrassment.

Cache placement requires a separate document of its own, and the interested reader will find one appended to this volume. For now, note only that a cache is only as useful as its description. "The oak by the stream" has proven inadequate documentation in at least seven expeditions this reporter is aware of. Bearings, paced distances from fixed landmarks, and a second confirming reference are the minimum. Bury deeper than comfort suggests and mark the depression with something that will survive rain and a full season of vegetation.

Table 9: Wilderness Random Encounters (d20)

Roll Encounter Terrain Notes
1 Wolf Pack (2d4) Forest, Hills Follows party until driven off or fed
2 Bandit Scouts (1d4) Roads, Plains Signal main band if not stopped
3 Flash Flood Ravine, Desert Lose 1d4 hours; roll for pack loss
4 Merchant Caravan Any Road
5 Diseased Livestock Plains, Farmland Exposes rations to contamination risk
6 Giant Spider (1d2) Forest, Cave
7 Unmarked Grave Site Any May indicate plague or recent battle
8 Toll Collectors (Dubious) Bridge, Ford Negotiation or combat; record outcome
9 Wild Boar (1d3) Forest, Scrub
10 Lost Pilgrim Any May carry useful local knowledge
11 Dense Fog Moor, Coast Navigation checks required; halved pace
12 Brigand Ambush (2d6) Roads, Forest Leader flees if half force is downed
13 Unstable Ground Swamp, Hills Mounts and carts at risk of injury
14 Refugee Column Plains, Roads
15 Venomous Serpent Desert, Scrub Antidote required within 6 hours
16 Bear, Brown (1) Forest, Mountain
17 Broken Bridge River, Road Add 1d6+2 hours for alternate crossing
18 Deserters (1d6) Any Armed; may bargain or attack
19 Wildfire Scar Plains, Forest Forage yields halved for next 2 days
20 No Encounter Any

Beyond supply and pace, the prudent planner must account for the unexpected. No march through open wilderness proceeds in perfect quiet. Beasts seek the same water sources as travelers, and not all creatures yield the trail to armed men. Outlaws read road signs as well as any merchant, and the desperate will gamble against odds that a calm observer would decline. A company that fixes its attention entirely on the next supply depot may find that attention interrupted before arrival.

The table below catalogs common hazards by frequency of occurrence. Roll one twenty-sided die once per day of travel, or twice on days involving river crossings, elevated passes, or known bandit roads. The Notes column records persistent effects or special conditions; entries left blank indicate encounters that resolve on contact with no lasting consequence.

Encounters rated at lower rolls tend toward the merely costly; those in the middle range carry the most variable outcomes, depending on party preparedness and luck of ground. High rolls are rewards for careful watch-keeping, route selection, and the slow accumulation of good practice that veterans call habit and novices call caution. Record each encounter in the march journal with terrain, time of day, and resolution, so that the same road may be mapped more accurately on the next passage.
